devanagari: सूत उवाच । अथर्ववित्सुमन्तुश्च शिष्यम् अध्यापयत् स्वकाम् । संहितां सोऽपि पथ्याय वेददर्शाय चोक्तवान् ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: sūta uvāca atharvavitsumantuśca śiṣyam adhyāpayat svakām saṃhitāṃ so

[nominative single from √ vac class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ vac class 3 verb]

translation:
Sūta said:
Sumantu was well versed in the Atharva Veda. He taught his collection (of Atharva hymns) to his disciple Kabandha[1]) who in his turn taught it to Pathya and Vedadarśa.

devanagari: शौक्लायनिर्ब्रह्मबलिः मादोषः पिप्पलायनिः । वेददर्शस्य शिष्यास्ते पथ्यशिष्यानथो श्रृणु । कुमुदः शुनको ब्रह्मन् जाजलिश्चाप्यथर्ववित् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: śauklāyanirbrahmabaliḥ mādoṣaḥ pippalāyaniḥ vedadarśasya śiṣyāste pathyaśiṣyānatho śrṛṇu kumudaḥ śunako brahman jājaliścāpyatharvavit
translation: The disciples of Vedadarśa were Śauklāyani, Brahmabali, Modoṣa and Pippalāyani. ( Vedadarśa divided the Saṃhita [Saṃhitā?] in four parts and taught them). Listen to the (names of) disciples of Pathya, O Brāhmaṇa (Śaunaka). Kumuda, Śunaka, and Jājali, the expert knower of the Atharva-Veda (were the pupils of Pathya who divided the Saṃhitā in three parts and taught them).

devanagari: बभ्रुः शिष्योऽथांगिरसः सैन्धवायन एव च । अधीयेतां संहिते द्वे सावर्णाद्यास्तथापरे ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: babhruḥ śiṣyo adhīyetāṃ saṃhite dve sāvarṇādyāstathāpare

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect middle third dual], [imperfect passive third dual]

translation: Śunaka (born in the family of Aṅgiras) had two disciples—Babhru as well as Saindhavāyana. They studied the two collections (of the Atharva) their disciples—Sāvarṇya and others also (studied the same in their turn).

devanagari: नक्षत्रकल्पः शान्तिश्च कश्यपाङ्गिरसादयः । एते आथर्वणाचार्याः श्रृणु पौराणिकान् मुने ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: nakṣatrakalpaḥ śāntiśca kaśyapāṅgirasādayaḥ ete ātharvaṇācāryāḥ śrṛṇu paurāṇikān mune
translation: Nakṣatra-Kalpa, Śānti-Kalpa[2], Kaśyapa, Aṅgiras and others were the teachers of the Atharva Veda. O Śaunaka, now listen to the names of the teachers of the Purāṇic lore.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
As Viṣṇu Purāṇa 3.6.13-14 states Nakṣatra-Kalpa, Śānti-Kalpa, Veda-Kalpa, Saṃhitā-Kalpa and Āṅgirasa Kalpa are the collection of Atharvan hymns and sages like Kaśyapa, Aṅgiras preserved them. These Kalpas treat of rituals connected with the worship of Nakṣatras, Śāntikarma (propitiatory rituals) etc. Here the names of the Saṃhitās stand for the authors (Bhāvāratha Dīpikā).

devanagari: त्रय्यारुणिः कश्यपश्च सावर्णिः अकृतव्रणः । वैशंपायनहारीतौ षड् वै पौराणिका इमे ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: trayyāruṇiḥ kaśyapaśca sāvarṇiḥ akṛtavraṇaḥ vaiśaṃpāyanahārītau ṣaḍ vai paurāṇikā ime

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: Traiyyāruṇi, Kāśyapa, Sāvarṇi, Akṛtavraṇa, Vaiśampāyana and Hārīta—these six are traditionally noted as the teachers of the Purāṇas.

devanagari: अधीयन्त व्यासशिष्यात् संहितां मत्पितुर्मुखात् । एकैकाम् अहमेतेषां शिष्यः सर्वाः समध्यगाम् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: adhīyanta vyāsaśiṣyāt saṃhitāṃ matpiturmukhāt ekaikām ahameteṣāṃ śiṣyaḥ sarvāḥ samadhyagām

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect middle third plural], [imperfect passive third plural]
[optative active third single], [benedictive active third single]
[imperfect active first single], [aorist active first single]

translation: These teachers learnt one Purāṇa collection each, by oral tradition, from my father (Romaharṣaṇa) who was a pupil of Vyāsa. While I, as a disciple of each of them, learnt them all.

devanagari: कश्यपोऽहं च सावर्णी रामशिष्योऽकृतव्रणः । अधीमहि व्यासशिष्यात् चत्वारो मूलसंहिताः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: kaśyapo adhīmahi vyāsaśiṣyāt catvāro mūlasaṃhitāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [optative active third single], [benedictive active third single]

translation: I, Kaśyapa, Sāvarṇi and Paraśurāma’s disciple Akṛtavraṇa—We four learnt the original four collections of Purāṇa from Vyāsa’s disciple.

devanagari: पुराणलक्षणं ब्रह्मन् ब्रह्मर्षिभिः निरूपितम् । श्रृणुष्व बुद्धिमाश्रित्य वेदशास्त्रानुसारतः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: purāṇalakṣaṇaṃ brahman brahmarṣibhiḥ nirūpitam śrṛṇuṣva buddhimāśritya vedaśāstrānusārataḥ
translation: Now listen with intelligent attention the characteristics of Purāṇas as described by Brāhmaṇa sages in conformity to Vedas and Śāstras.

devanagari: सर्गोऽस्याथ विसर्गश्च वृत्तिरक्षान्तराणि च । वंशो वंशानुचरितं संस्था हेतुरपाश्रयः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: sargo vaṃśo vaṃśānucaritaṃ saṃsthā heturapāśrayaḥ
translation: Those who are experts in Purāṇic lore declare that a Purāṇa is distinguished by the following ten characteristics[3] (or topics dealt with): (1) Subtle creation (Sarga), (2) gross creation (Visarga), (3) Sustenance (Vṛtti), (4) Protection of the universe (Rakṣā), (5) Cause or the lust for Karmas (Hetu), (6) the periods of Manu (Antara i.e. Manvantara), (7) Dynasties of Kings (Vaṃśa), (8) Deeds of the Lord and dynastic kings (Vaṃśānucarita), (9) Physical annihilation (Saṃsthā) (10) The Last support or ultimate reality (Apāśraya). O Brāhmaṇa (Śaunaka), some say that Purāṇas have five characteristics[4] which distinguishes between great and small Purāṇas.[5]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Bhāgavata Purāṇa Supra 2.10.3-7 has given these ten characteristics with some different nomenclature. For the discussion of these topics vide Vol. I. Introduction, pp. XVIII-XXXIV.

Footnote [4]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā enumerates them as follows: Sarga, Pratisarga, Vaṃśa, manvantara and Vaṃśānucarita.

Footnote [5]:
As the Intro, mentioned in the above note shows these ten characteristics can be epitomised into five. Hence these need not be regarded as a differentia between a Mahā-Purāṇa and Upa-Purāṇa.

devanagari: दशभिः लक्षणैर्युक्तं पुराणं तद्विदो विदुः । केचित्पञ्चविधं ब्रह्मन् महदल्पव्यवस्थया ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: daśabhiḥ lakṣaṇairyuktaṃ purāṇaṃ tadvido viduḥ kecitpañcavidhaṃ brahman mahadalpavyavasthayā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ yuj class 7 verb], [accusative single from √ yuj class 7 verb]

translation: Those who are experts in Purāṇic lore declare that a Purāṇa is distinguished by the following ten characteristics[3] (or topics dealt with): (1) Subtle creation (Sarga), (2) gross creation (Visarga), (3) Sustenance (Vṛtti), (4) Protection of the universe (Rakṣā), (5) Cause or the lust for Karmas (Hetu), (6) the periods of Manu (Antara i.e. Manvantara), (7) Dynasties of Kings (Vaṃśa), (8) Deeds of the Lord and dynastic kings (Vaṃśānucarita), (9) Physical annihilation (Saṃsthā) (10) The Last support or ultimate reality (Apāśraya). O Brāhmaṇa (Śaunaka), some say that Purāṇas have five characteristics[4] which distinguishes between great and small Purāṇas.[5]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Bhāgavata Purāṇa Supra 2.10.3-7 has given these ten characteristics with some different nomenclature. For the discussion of these topics vide Vol. I. Introduction, pp. XVIII-XXXIV.

Footnote [4]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā enumerates them as follows: Sarga, Pratisarga, Vaṃśa, manvantara and Vaṃśānucarita.

Footnote [5]:
As the Intro, mentioned in the above note shows these ten characteristics can be epitomised into five. Hence these need not be regarded as a differentia between a Mahā-Purāṇa and Upa-Purāṇa.

devanagari: अव्याकृतगुणक्षोभान् महतस्त्रिवृतोऽहमः । भूतसूक्ष्मेन्द्रियार्थानां संभवः सर्ग उच्यते ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: avyākṛtaguṇakṣobhān mahatastrivṛto bhūtasūkṣmendriyārthānāṃ saṃbhavaḥ sarga ucyate
translation: Sarga (Subtle creation) is (i.e. consists of topics like) the evolution of Mahat (the principle of cosmic Intelligence) from the agitation of and disturbance in the balance of equilibrium of the three guṇas of the Unmanifest Prakṛti—the Primordial Matter) leading to the evolution of three-fold Ahaṃkāra (of the Vaikārika or Sāttvika, Rājasa and Tāmasa types, according to the dominance of the particular guṇa) the evolutes of which are subtle elements (bhūtas), the sense-organs and their objects (viz. gross elements and the deities presiding over the sense-organs).

devanagari: पुरुषानुगृहीतानाम् एतेषां वासनामयः । विसर्गोऽयं समाहारो बीजाद्बीजं चराचरम् ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: puruṣānugṛhītānām eteṣāṃ vāsanāmayaḥ visargo
translation: Visarga (gross creation) is the creation of the gross mobile and immobile nature resulting from the collective effects evolved out of Mahat and other principles charged with the potency of creation by the Supreme Person and dominated by the influence of previous Karmas (of the Jīvas) evolving in the manner of the continuation of a seed from a seed.

devanagari: वृत्तिर्भूतानि भूतानां चराणाम् अचराणि च । कृता स्वेन नृणां तत्र कामात् चोदनयापि वा ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: vṛttirbhūtāni bhūtānāṃ carāṇām acarāṇi ca kṛtā svena nṛṇāṃ tatra kāmāt codanayāpi vā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

translation: The immobile creation (e.g. food-grains, fruits, roots) and some products of mobile creation also form the sustenance of the mobile creation. And in the case of human beings, the sustenance is determined by their nature, desire and scriptural injunctions[6] (the earth is the real supporter sustenance of mobile creatures as described in the fifth Skandha.
purport:
Footnote [6]:
Padaratnāvalī The sustenance is determined according to the will of men (e.g. fruits, grain, etc.) and to the prescriptions laid down in the Śāstras (e.g. caru puroḍāśa).

devanagari: रक्षाच्युतावतारेहा विश्वस्यानु युगे युगे । तिर्यङ्मर्त्यर्षिदेवेषु हन्यन्ते यैस्त्रयीद्विषः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: rakṣācyutāvatārehā viśvasyānu yuge yuge tiryaṅmartyarṣideveṣu hanyante yaistrayīdviṣaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single], [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single], [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single], [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: Rakṣā or Protection of the universe consists of the incarnations and the sportive acts (exploits, etc.) of Lord Viṣṇu, from age to age appearing among sub-human beings (e.g. the Boar incarnation), human beings (e.g. Paraśurāma, Rāma) sages (e.g. Kapila) and among gods, and the extermination of the haters of the three Vedas (e.g. demons).

devanagari: मन्वन्तरं मनुर्देवा मनुपुत्राः सुरेश्वराः । ऋषयोंऽशावताराश्च हरेः षड्विधमुच्यते ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: manvantaraṃ manurdevā manuputrāḥ sureśvarāḥ ṛṣayoṃ
translation: Manvantara (Manu-period) is the period presided over by the particular Manu (e.g. Vaivasvata Manu at present), the set of gods, the sons of Manu, Indra, seven sages and the incarnations of Hari. Thus a Manvantara is characterized by the above six special features.

devanagari: राज्ञां ब्रह्मप्रसूतानां वंशस्त्रैकालिकोऽन्वयः । वंशानुचरितं तेषां वृत्तं वंशधराश्च ये ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: rājñāṃ brahmaprasūtānāṃ vaṃśastraikāliko vaṃśānucaritaṃ teṣāṃ vṛttaṃ vaṃśadharāśca ye

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ vṛt class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ vṛt class 1 verb]

translation: Vaṃśa (race) is the line or race of pure, blue-blooded kings born of god Brahmā and continued throughout the three divisions of time (viz. the past, present and future). Vaṃśānucarita is the history of these kings, and of their descendants.

devanagari: नैमित्तिकः प्राकृतिको नित्य आत्यन्तिको लयः । संस्थेति कविभिः प्रोक्तः चतुर्धास्य स्वभावतः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: naimittikaḥ prākṛtiko nitya ātyantiko layaḥ saṃstheti kavibhiḥ proktaḥ caturdhāsya svabhāvataḥ
translation: The destruction of the universe created by Māyā[7] is of four types (1) Occasional or brought about by a particular cause, (2) Prākṛtika (of the Prakṛti), (3) Constant and (4) Absolute. This dissolution is called Saṃsthā by the sages.
purport:
Footnote [7]:
The dissolution brought about by the Māyā.

devanagari: हेतुर्जीवोऽस्य सर्गादेः अविद्याकर्मकारकः । यं चानुशायिनं प्राहुः अव्याकृतमुतापरे ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: heturjīvo yaṃ cānuśāyinaṃ prāhuḥ avyākṛtamutāpare
translation: [8] The Hetu (cause), the motive power or cause of the universe is the Jīva (individual soul). He is the doer of actions under the influence of Nescience or ignorance. From the point of those who emphasize his conscious or spiritual aspect, he is the enjoyer of the fruits of the balance of his Karmas, while from that of those who stress the Upādhi (conditioned existence) aspect, he is unmanifest i.e. without name and form before the creation.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā Through his ignorance Jīva commits acts both of merits and sins. It is for enjoying the fruits of those Karmas that the world is there. Thus he becomes the cause of the creation. Some opine that Jīva has always a balance of Karmas to be enjoyed (bhukta-phala-karmāvaśeṣa [karmāvaśeṣaḥ]) and is full of desire to enjoy, while others say that before creation he had no name or form but possessed the impressions of Karmas (Vāsanā).
Padaratnāvalī Interprets anuśāyin as ‘one who enters into Paramātman at the time of deluge and sleeps and avyākṛta is disinterested (?) or immutable (nirvikāra) like the sky. The world is created for ths Jīvas to experience pleasure or pain as per their Karmas. Hence he is the cause of creation.

devanagari: व्यतिरेकान्वयो यस्य जाग्रत् स्वप्नसुषुप्तिषु । मायामयेषु तद्ब्रह्म जीववृत्तिष्वपाश्रयः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: vyatirekānvayo yasya jāgrat svapnasuṣuptiṣu māyāmayeṣu tadbrahma jīvavṛttiṣvapāśrayaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb]
[nominative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb]

translation: [9] Apāśraya (The last support or ultimate reality) is Brahman. It is present as a witness to what is effected by Māyā, as in the three states of Jīva (viz. Viśva, Taijasa and Prājña) in wakefulness, dreaming and dreamless-deep-sleep and is also quite different or distinct from them as in Samādhi. Just as the basic matter (e.g. clay) is both related to and unrelated to its products (clay is found in all things made of clay and still it exists apart from them); or just as Sat (real existence) forms the substratum of the name and form, Brahman is related to all the. (nine) stages[10] of living beings since their inception to death (as the substratum) and is distinct from them. (The Apāśraya is the highest goal of these characteristics of Purāṇas).
purport:
Footnote [9]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā: Brahman is present in all the products of Prakṛti and the states of Jīva but is not affected by it essentially. Brahman is unconnected.
Padaratnāvalī: Viṣṇu is the only support (Apāśraya) and none else. He is present in all stales of Jīva but is not affected by them. He exists before, during and after these states (such as wakefulness and others). Brahman is both connected and unconnected with all states and substances. And hence their support. It, being without a second, is inferrable by invariable concomitance (anvaya) or the reverse of it (Vyatireka).

Footnote [10]:
They are as follows: Entry into the womb (as a seed) gestation, birth, infancy (upto the age of five), boyhood (upto sixteen), youth (upto forty-five), middle age (upto sixty), old age and death—vide Supra 11.22.46.

devanagari: पदार्थेषु यथा द्रव्यं सन्मात्रं रूपनामसु । बीजादिपञ्चतान्तासु ह्यवस्थासु युतायुतम् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: padārtheṣu yathā dravyaṃ sanmātraṃ rūpanāmasu bījādipañcatāntāsu hyavasthāsu yutāyutam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [vocative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ yu class 9 verb]
[vocative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ yu class 9 verb]
[nominative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [nominative single from √ yu class 9 verb]

translation: [9] Apāśraya (The last support or ultimate reality) is Brahman. It is present as a witness to what is effected by Māyā, as in the three states of Jīva (viz. Viśva, Taijasa and Prājña) in wakefulness, dreaming and dreamless-deep-sleep and is also quite different or distinct from them as in Samādhi. Just as the basic matter (e.g. clay) is both related to and unrelated to its products (clay is found in all things made of clay and still it exists apart from them); or just as Sat (real existence) forms the substratum of the name and form, Brahman is related to all the. (nine) stages[10] of living beings since their inception to death (as the substratum) and is distinct from them. (The Apāśraya is the highest goal of these characteristics of Purāṇas).
purport:
Footnote [9]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā: Brahman is present in all the products of Prakṛti and the states of Jīva but is not affected by it essentially. Brahman is unconnected.
Padaratnāvalī: Viṣṇu is the only support (Apāśraya) and none else. He is present in all stales of Jīva but is not affected by them. He exists before, during and after these states (such as wakefulness and others). Brahman is both connected and unconnected with all states and substances. And hence their support. It, being without a second, is inferrable by invariable concomitance (anvaya) or the reverse of it (Vyatireka).

Footnote [10]:
They are as follows: Entry into the womb (as a seed) gestation, birth, infancy (upto the age of five), boyhood (upto sixteen), youth (upto forty-five), middle age (upto sixty), old age and death—vide Supra 11.22.46.

devanagari: विरमेत यदा चित्तं हित्वा वृत्तित्रयं स्वयम् । योगेन वा तदात्मानं वेदेहाया निवर्तते ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: virameta yadā cittaṃ hitvā vṛttitrayaṃ svayam yogena vā tadātmānaṃ vedehāyā nivartate

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative single from √ man class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 8 verb]
[nominative single from √ man class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ man class 8 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 8 verb]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: When the mind transcends the (above-mentioned) three states (of Jīva) either of its own accord (as in the case of sages like Vāmadeva who realized that this creation is Māyā and Brahman is the only reality)—or by the practice of Yoga (as with Kapila’s mother Devahūtī), then a person realizes Ātman (the self) (ceases to have any Vāsanās) and desists from worldly activities.

devanagari: एवं लक्षणलक्ष्याणि पुराणानि पुराविदः । मुनयोऽष्टादश प्राहुः क्षुल्लकानि महान्ति च ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ lakṣaṇalakṣyāṇi purāṇāni purāvidaḥ munayo
translation: Sages who are well versed in ancient lore declare that eighteen Purāṇas whether big or small are distinguished by the ten characteristics (described above).

devanagari: ब्राह्मं पाद्मं वैष्णवं च शैवं लैङ्गं सगारुडं । नारदीयं भागवतं आग्नेयं स्कान्दसंज्ञितम् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: brāhmaṃ pādmaṃ vaiṣṇavaṃ ca śaivaṃ laiṅgaṃ sagāruḍaṃ nāradīyaṃ bhāgavataṃ āgneyaṃ skāndasaṃjñitam
translation: The names of the eighteen Purāṇas are: (1) Brahma, (2) Padma, (3) Viṣṇu, (4) Śiva, (5) Liṅga, (6) Garuḍa, (7) Nārada, (8) Bhāgavata, (9) Agni, (10) Skanda, (11) Bhaviṣya, (12) Brahmavaivarta, (13) Mārkaṇḍeya (14) Vāmana (15) Varāha, (16) Matsya (17) Kūrma and (18) Brahmāṇḍa.

devanagari: भविष्यं ब्रह्मवैवर्तं मार्कण्डेयं सवामनम् । वाराहं मात्स्यं कौर्मं च ब्रह्माण्डाख्यमिति त्रिषट् ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: bhaviṣyaṃ brahmavaivartaṃ mārkaṇḍeyaṃ savāmanam vārāhaṃ mātsyaṃ kaurmaṃ ca brahmāṇḍākhyamiti triṣaṭ
translation: The names of the eighteen Purāṇas are: (1) Brahma, (2) Padma, (3) Viṣṇu, (4) Śiva, (5) Liṅga, (6) Garuḍa, (7) Nārada, (8) Bhāgavata, (9) Agni, (10) Skanda, (11) Bhaviṣya, (12) Brahmavaivarta, (13) Mārkaṇḍeya (14) Vāmana (15) Varāha, (16) Matsya (17) Kūrma and (18) Brahmāṇḍa.

devanagari: ब्रह्मन्निदं समाख्यातं शाखाप्रणयनं मुनेः । शिष्यशिष्यप्रशिष्याणां ब्रह्मतेजोविवर्धनम् ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: brahmannidaṃ samākhyātaṃ śākhāpraṇayanaṃ muneḥ śiṣyaśiṣyapraśiṣyāṇāṃ brahmatejovivardhanam
translation:
O Brāhmaṇa sage (Śaunaka): Thus has been related (to you) the account of the classification of Vedas into different branches by the sage (Vyāsa) and his disciples and pupils of those disciples. It (listening to this account) enhances the glory of Brahman (of the listeners, if devoutly and attentively heard.)
